text.title: Review Health Metrics
text.summary: Five review health metrics: time to first review, cycle time, rework rate, comment ratio, defect escape rate — what they measure and how to act on them.

rule.statement: Track five review health metrics: time to first review (target under 4 hours), review cycle time (target under 24 hours total), rework rate (target under 20% of PRs needing multiple iterations), comment ratio (comments per 100 lines changed), defect escape rate (production bugs that passed review).
rule.rationale: These five metrics cover speed (time to first review, cycle time), quality (rework rate, defect escape), and noise (comment ratio). Tracking only speed metrics drives rubber-stamp approvals; tracking only quality metrics misses throughput bottlenecks.

rule.statement: Measure time from PR "ready for review" event to first substantive comment. Filter out draft-to-ready transitions and bot auto-comments — both inflate the metric without reflecting real review latency.
rule.rationale: Time to first review is the metric developers feel most directly. Long wait times (over 8 hours) are the primary cause of merge bottlenecks and context-switching cost when developers return to a PR after a day. Bot auto-comments at second 0 create false good numbers while reviewers still haven't looked.

rule.statement: Rework rate = percentage of PRs requiring more than one review iteration after the first substantive human review. Spikes in rework rate signal unclear specs or weak agent precision, not author incompetence.
rule.rationale: High rework rate is a system problem, not an individual problem. When rework spikes, investigate: were requirements clear before the PR was opened? Are agent BLOCKING comments reliable? Are reviewers consistent? Optimize the system, not the individuals.

rule.statement: Track production bugs that were present in reviewed code. Segment by bug class (correctness, security, performance) to identify which review categories have poor coverage.
rule.rationale: Defect escape rate is the ultimate measure of review quality. High escape rate in security bugs means security review is inadequate. High escape rate in performance bugs means performance checks are missing or unreliable. Use escape rate to direct investment in review improvement.

rule.statement: Track the ratio of BLOCKING to total comments. A healthy ratio is 15-25% BLOCKING. Below 5% means reviews are not catching real issues. Above 40% means the author is submitting unready PRs or the agent is miscalibrated toward false positives.
rule.rationale: Comment ratio alone is misleading — 50 nitpicks on a 10-line PR looks like thorough review but is noise. The BLOCKING ratio reveals whether the review is finding real issues or generating noise. Calibrate the agent toward the target blocking ratio by adjusting the review prompt based on precision tracking.
